urn:contentItem:6JJV-FCW3-S16S-4508-00000-00May 04, 2026: Red Apple Expands Media Footprint with Worldwide Audio News Network2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:04ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: Red Apple Expands Media Footprint with Worldwide Audio News Network

Red Apple Group Inc. unveiled The Worldwide News Network, a global audio news platform, as part of its push to broaden its media footprint. The launch was disclosed on 4th May 2026 by John Catsimatidis, owner and CEO of Red Apple Media.

Lee Harris, a National Radio Hall of Fame inductee, will serve as Vice President of News for WABC Radio and The Worldwide News Network. Harris, who spent three decades as a morning newscaster at 1010 WINS in New York, brings extensive experience to the project.

The company explained that The Worldwide News Network builds on WABC Radio’s existing reach, which spans all 50 states and 178 countries through multiple distribution channels. Chad Lopez, president of Red Apple Media and WABC Radio, noted that audio news is no longer constrained by geography or time zones and that the platform will provide timely, credible reporting.

Harris commented that the initiative reflects the founder’s inclination toward bold, forward-looking projects and is intended to create a news organization with both national and international strength.

SECTION 1 RED APPLE GROUP INC PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Red Apple Group Inc, founded in 1970 by John Catsimatidis, is a privately-held conglomerate headquartered in New York City. Employing over 8,000 people, it operates in energy, real estate, and retail, with annual revenues exceeding $5 billion. Its Gristedes and DAgostino supermarkets serve New York, while United Refining Company processes 70,000 barrels of oil daily in Pennsylvania. Red Apples real estate arm manages 2 million square feet of commercial and residential properties. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy investments, focusing on solar projects. The companys culture emphasizes entrepreneurship, community engagement, and employee loyalty, with training in retail and energy operations. Competing with Hess and Stop & Shop, Red Apple stands out for its diversified portfolio and regional focus. Recent initiatives include digital retail enhancements and sustainable fuel production, aligning with market trends. Catsimatidis leadership drives its growth in dynamic sectors.

value.Title: May 04, 2026: Red Apple Media Launches Global Audio News Platform Led by WABC Veteran Lee Harris

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK1-DYP3-S685-04BH-00000-00May 04, 2026: Red Apple Media Launches Global Audio News Platform Led by WABC Veteran Lee Harris2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:04ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: Red Apple Media Launches Global Audio News Platform Led by WABC Veteran Lee Harris

Red Apple Group Inc. announced the creation of The Worldwide News Network, a global audio news platform, as part of its strategy to expand its media footprint. The plan was unveiled on 4th May 2026 by John Catsimatidis, owner and CEO of Red Apple Media.

Lee Harris, a National Radio Hall of Fame inductee, will serve as Vice President of News for WABC Radio and The Worldwide News Network. Harris brings 30 years of experience as a morning newscaster at 1010 WINS in New York.

The move underscores Red Apple Media's goal to fuse traditional radio strength with digital distribution. The company noted that WABC Radio already reaches all 50 states and 178 countries through a variety of channels, and the Worldwide News Network is designed to broaden access further.

Chad Lopez, president of Red Apple Media and WABC Radio, said audio news can be consumed instantly anywhere, regardless of signal strength or time zone, and that the platform will offer credible reporting around the clock.

Harris described the project as a transformative, ambitious venture that aligns with the founder’s history of bold bets, and he said the team aims to build a global audio news operation with both national and international reach.

INDEX

SECTION 1 RED APPLE GROUP INC PROFILE

SECTION 2 RED APPLE GROUP INC RANKING

SECTION 1 RED APPLE GROUP INC PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Red Apple Group Inc, founded in 1970 by John Catsimatidis, is a privately-held conglomerate headquartered in New York City. Employing over 8,000 people, it operates in energy, real estate, and retail, with annual revenues exceeding $5 billion. Its Gristedes and DAgostino supermarkets serve New York, while United Refining Company processes 70,000 barrels of oil daily in Pennsylvania. Red Apples real estate arm manages 2 million square feet of commercial and residential properties. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy investments, focusing on solar projects. The companys culture emphasizes entrepreneurship, community engagement, and employee loyalty, with training in retail and energy operations. Competing with Hess and Stop & Shop, Red Apple stands out for its diversified portfolio and regional focus. Recent initiatives include digital retail enhancements and sustainable fuel production, aligning with market trends. Catsimatidis leadership drives its growth in dynamic sectors.

1.2 SUMMARY

urn:contentItem:6JJN-FNH3-S0MP-42RV-00000-00May 04, 2026: Red Apple Expands Global Reach with New Audio News Network2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:02:04ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: Red Apple Expands Global Reach with New Audio News Network

In a significant move to enhance its media presence, Red Apple Group Inc. has announced the launch of The Worldwide News Network, a new global audio news platform. This initiative was revealed on 4th May 2026 by John Catsimatidis, the Owner and CEO of Red Apple Media.

To lead this ambitious project, Red Apple Media has appointed Lee Harris, a National Radio Hall of Fame inductee, as the Vice President of News for WABC Radio and The Worldwide News Network. Harris, known for his impressive 30-year tenure as morning newscaster at 1010 WINS/New York, brings a wealth of experience to the role.

"Today’s digital technology gives us the power to deliver the truth instantly, not just across America, but across Europe and around the globe," said Catsimatidis. He emphasized that WABC Radio is already accessible in all 50 states and in 178 countries through various distribution channels, making The Worldwide News Network a logical progression in their goal to offer reliable news.

"Audio news consumption is no longer confined by geography, signal strength, or time zone," added Chad Lopez, President of Red Apple Media and WABC Radio. He stressed the importance of providing listeners with immediate access to credible reporting at any time and from anywhere. With Harris at the helm, Lopez expressed confidence in developing this operation into a formidable global audio news platform.

Harris remarked on the transformative nature of this venture, stating, "John Catsimatidis has never thought small, and this venture is no exception. The Worldwide News Network is an ambitious, forward-looking news platform designed for how people consume information today." He expressed his excitement to join Red Apple Media and WABC Radio at this pivotal moment and contribute to establishing a news organization with both national and international strength.

Red Apple Group Inc, founded in 1970 by John Catsimatidis, is a privately-held conglomerate headquartered in New York City. Employing over 8,000 people, it operates in energy, real estate, and retail, with annual revenues exceeding $5 billion. Its Gristedes and DAgostino supermarkets serve New York, while United Refining Company processes 70,000 barrels of oil daily in Pennsylvania. Red Apples real estate arm manages 2 million square feet of commercial and residential properties. In 2024, it expanded renewable energy investments, focusing on solar projects. The companys culture emphasizes entrepreneurship, community engagement, and employee loyalty, with training in retail and energy operations. Competing with Hess and Stop & Shop, Red Apple stands out for its diversified portfolio and regional focus. Recent initiatives include digital retail enhancements and sustainable fuel production, aligning with market trends. Catsimatidis leadership drives its growth in dynamic sectors.

value.Title: May 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-CFP3-S020-J528-00000-00May 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS

On February 25, 2026, Consigli Construction Co. commenced work on the Williams College Museum of Art, marking the first purpose-built facility on the campus. Abigail Berkebile, a prominent figure at the company, noted the complex and unique design of the project, highlighting the thrill of witnessing the museum staff's enthusiasm as the building progresses. Berkebile also utilized the project's visibility to emphasize the diverse opportunities available for women in the industry, explaining that project teams rely heavily on expertise in accounting, IT, legal, marketing, and design, allowing women to contribute meaningfully without necessarily requiring a formal construction background. To maintain focus during the project, she highlighted a routine of organizing her calendar and setting aside time to connect with her team daily to foster collaboration.

In other industry developments, Suffolk Construction is making steady progress on a major interchange project on U.S. Route 58 near downtown Suffolk, Virginia. Effective May 5, 2026, the Virginia Department of Transportation began removing a long-term lane closure, allowing all three lanes of westbound traffic to flow freely and facilitating a new ramp and flyover for eastbound traffic. In the renewable energy space, Virgin Media O2 announced a 10-year Power Purchase Agreement on May 1, 2026, to source energy from Egg Power's 70MW solar farm in Suffolk, which is currently under construction and set to generate power by 2027. Mark Hardman, Director of Finance Operations at VMO2, stated, "This agreement with Egg Power is the latest step in Virgin Media O2's journey to achieve net zero emissions by the end of 2040. We're committed to growing and operating our business in a way that's good for people and the planet."

Meanwhile, Turner Construction Company achieved a significant milestone on April 28, 2026, with the topping out of the 72-megawatt Aligned IAD-06 Data Center in Frederick, Maryland. The 450,000-square-foot facility logged over 80,000 craft hours with approximately 345 skilled workers. Nick Sevier, Operations Manager at Turner, remarked, "Topping out this facility represents more than completing the structural frame. It reflects the strength of our partnership with Aligned, the dedication of our trade partners, and Turner's commitment to delivering this complex, high-performance data center safely and efficiently." On the same day, Turner also announced the start of vertical construction on the $900 million, 600,000-square-foot expansion of the Geisinger Wyoming Valley Medical Center in Pennsylvania, which will increase the hospital's capacity to 500 beds by the first quarter of 2028. David Kaminski, Vice President & Senior General Manager, noted, "This milestone marks an important step forward in delivering expanded, modern health care access for families across northeastern Pennsylvania."

Elsewhere in the sector, Gilbane Development announced on April 28, 2026, the appointment of Andreas Vlahakis as head of capital markets. Operating from New York City, Vlahakis will focus on refining funding strategies and executing ground-up multifamily and student housing investments, reporting directly to President and CEO James Patchett. Additionally, on April 3, 2026, Gilbane began leasing Phase II of its $135 million affordable housing redevelopment at Barnaby & 7th in Washington, D.C. The second phase includes 229 units across four buildings slated for completion by the summer of 2026, with twenty percent of the apartments designated as Permanent Supportive Housing managed by the district's Department of Homeless Services.

value.Title: Lancaster County farmland advocates and farmers should find middle ground on solar energy [editorial]

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ2-1W63-RTBY-S2RF-00000-00Lancaster County farmland advocates and farmers should find middle ground on solar energy [editorial]2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisLancaster County farmland advocates and farmers should find middle ground on solar energy [editorial]THE LNPLANCASTERONLINE EDITORIAL BOARD

THE ISSUE

A debate over solar panels is pitting farmland preservationists in Lancaster County against proponents of renewable energy and farmers seeking ways to help sustain their family farms. At issue is whether the installation of solar panels on farm property is considered an agricultural use.

Farming in the United States — even in the prized soil of Lancaster County — has always been a tough occupation.

It’s been made tougher by the escalation in the prices of fertilizer and diesel fuel.

In recent years, some farmers have turned to solar power. To make money or reduce energy costs, they’ve welcomed onto their farmland what are called solar arrays — collections of solar panels installed together to generate electricity from sunlight.

As LNP | LancasterOnline reported, “In 2023, Boston-based solar developer New Leaf Energy and property owners Gerald and Jewel Gruber sought permission from the West Lampeter Township Zoning Hearing Board to build a 25-acre installation of solar panels on their farmland. ... The property owners tried to argue that the solar array qualifies as agriculture because of the plan to raise sheep underneath them.”

The property owners lost at the township zoning board and lost in a ruling from the Court of Common Pleas, affirmed in January by the Commonwealth Court on appeal. The ruling concluded that a solar array on top of a sheep pasture did not conform to West Lampeter Township’s rules for agricultural land use.

This pleased Jeff Swinehart, Lancaster Farmland Trust’s president and CEO.

“When we’re sitting on the most productive nonirrigated soils here in Lancaster County and we’re within a day’s drive to large metro areas with big populations, it just seems that the best use (of that land) is food or fiber rather than producing energy,” Swinehart told this newspaper.

Daniel Dotterer, a Clinton County farmer who testified in the case as the shepherd who would have grazed sheep on the property, was not so pleased. He said that putting solar arrays on agricultural land offers an opportunity for multigenerational family farmers to sustain their businesses, while also preserving farmland.

“Ninety-nine percent of all development is permanent,” Dotterer noted. “You put in a house, you put a Walmart, a Dollar General, that land is gone forever. The beauty of solar is it’s all removable.”

Both sides in this debate share the admirable aim of preserving farmland while sustaining family farms. Farmland preservation is particularly critical in Lancaster County, where vast acres of arable land have been lost to development.

But we were struck by this statistic: Pennsylvania ranks 49th in the nation when it comes to how much of its total energy production comes from renewable energy, according to Department of Energy data.

This is shockingly poor, especially as Pennsylvania was the first state to adopt modern renewable energy standards.

Renewable energy has been aggressively discouraged by the pro-fossil fuel Trump administration. From its perspective, concerns about climate change are gone with the wind, and the windmills.

This is a complete denial of reality.

The fact is that whether we acknowledge it, the climate is changing. And our reliance on fossil fuels is accelerating that change. The hot-one-day-cold-the-next temperature swings we’re experiencing this spring are yet more evidence of this.

Jennifer Francis, senior scientist at the Woodwell Climate Research Center in Massachusetts, recently told the Bloomberg Green Daily newsletter that studies suggest these “whiplash events” will “become more common in the future.”

Farmers have had to deal with the extreme weather brought by climate change in recent years. And they’ll have to deal with whatever it brings — drought, flooding, shifting growing seasons, vector-borne diseases, etc. — in the years to come.

Even if they don’t acknowledge climate science, farmers tend to be practical people. They see rising energy costs spurring some of the increasing demand for solar power.

This is why some Lancaster County farmers have installed roof-mounted solar arrays on barns and other buildings to offset their energy bills, a practice dubbed “net-metering.” The solar power generated is calculated against their energy bills; excess energy can be sold off through the grid.

This is a sensible and climate-friendly way to reduce energy costs. And yet some people object to solar arrays because they think solar panels are ugly. Or folks have been led, inaccurately, to believe that solar panels cause cancer. Such myths have been perpetuated by some within the fossil fuel industry and its lobby.

We’d like to see Lancaster County farmers, preservationists and other residents find some middle ground; perhaps that would mean determining, with zoning officials, what percentage of a farm’s acreage could hold solar arrays. Because fossil fuels are harming the planet. And the overwhelming demand for artificial intelligence is leading to the construction of energy-sucking data centers. That energy has to come from somewhere. Why not the sun?

In a letter to the editor published this weekend, Lancaster farmer Aidan Fife makes a case for agrivoltaics, the use of land for both agriculture and solar energy generation.

“I would argue that solar grazing is one of the easiest environmental and economic win-wins for preserving farmland and producing energy,” Fife wrote. “Solar farms require mowing and maintenance to keep the grass down. Sheep require grass! And sheep also benefit from the shade of solar panels during hot months. Agrivoltaics ... isn’t opposed to farmland preservation; it supports it.”

He added: “At a time when data centers will start taking over vast amounts of (Lancaster County’s) energy, and farmland is under threat, why close off an avenue to locally grown energy, food and additional farm revenue to keep land from being developed?”

As LNP | LancasterOnline reported, it may be difficult at this point to grow crops under solar panels — cost-effectiveness and commercial viability remain in question — but grazing sheep under solar panels seems like a reasonable way for a farmer to earn additional revenue.

Lancaster County isn’t likely to be blanketed in solar panels because land here is pricey and farms tend to be smaller than elsewhere. So we think some flexibility is called for in cases in which solar arrays make sense for farmers.

In 1986, a song by the English indie band The Housemartins contained the lyric, “It’s sheep we’re up against.”

In 2026, it’s not sheep we’re up against. It’s old ideas about energy and land use. It’s time for some fresh thinking to save our planet and our farmland.

value.Title: Hershey companies form alliance to deliver turnkey battery energy storage solutions

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JMD-7NH3-S0KW-Y41H-00000-00Hershey companies form alliance to deliver turnkey battery energy storage solutions2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisHershey companies form alliance to deliver turnkey battery energy storage solutionsEd GruverHarrisburgFULL TEXT

Two Hershey-based companies announced an alliancetodeliverturnkeybatteryenergystoragesolutions.ARM Group, headquartered in Hershey,is teamingwithHershey-basedBatricity, a specialized battery energy storage system design and integration firm. The alliance is aimed atdeliveringfully designed, installed, and commissioned energy storage solutions across North America.According to a release, therelationship brings together two organizations at a time when utilities, developers, and industrial clients are navigating growing energy reliability challenges, grid constraints, and the transition to more resilient and sustainable energy systems.“This alliance enhances ARM’s ability to support clients in the rapidly evolving energy market,” ARM Group Enterprises Inc. President & CEO Bryan Wehler said in a statement. “By aligning Batricity’s deep expertise in energy storage systems with ARM’s full-service engineering, permitting and construction capabilities, we are uniquely positioned to offer clients a more integrated and flexible approach to developing BESS projects in the evolving energy landscape.”ARM Group andBatricitywill collaborate on commercial, industrial, and utility-scale energy storage projects, with potential applications including grid resiliency, renewable integration, microgrids, peak shaving, EV charging infrastructure, and broader energy optimization initiatives.The collaboration is intended to reduce project risk and improve coordination across project phases, while allowing each organization tocontributeits respectiveexpertisebased on project needs, per the release.“This alliance allows us to pair our industry-leading supply chain and deep technical expertise together with proven design-build execution,” said Travis Eckert, CEO &co-founder ofBatricity. “We are excited to collaborate with ARM Group to deliver high-performance energy storage solutions that meet the growing demands of the market.”ARM Group LLC is a multidisciplinary engineering and science firm providing civil, environmental, electrical, and construction services across energy, infrastructure, and industrial markets. As part of ARM Group Enterprises Inc., ARM Group delivers integrated solutions from feasibility through construction, with a strong focus on safety, quality, and client success. The firm’s Energy & Power practice supports projects including power generation, transmission and distribution, renewable energy, and battery energy storage systems.Batricityis anenergy storage design and integration firm delivering turnkey energy storage solutions across commercial & industrial, utility-scale, and EV charging markets.The companyserves astheenergy storage partner for project developers, EPCs, ESCOs, and facility owners.Batricityis also the creator of Geometrics, a proprietary site intelligence platform.

value.Title: Microsoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand Surge

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-FN43-S35T-Y3YW-00000-00Microsoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand Surge2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisMicrosoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand SurgeBW Online Bureau

Rapid expansion of AI infrastructure and data centres is increasing pressure on Microsoft's long-term renewable energy commitments

Microsoft is considering revising its 2030 renewable energy target as rising electricity demand from artificial intelligence and data centres puts pressure on its climate commitments, according to a Bloomberg report.

The report said the rapid growth of energy-intensive artificial intelligence infrastructure and data centres is reshaping the feasibility of climate commitments that were made before the current AI expansion. Discussions within the company are said to be ongoing, and no final decision has been taken.

A Microsoft spokesperson said the company continued to explore opportunities to maintain its renewable energy matching goal. The spokesperson pointed to recently signed agreements with We Eneries to support 1.2 gigawatts of carbon-free energy projects in Wisconsin, including solar and battery storage facilities expected to begin operations from late 2028.

Like several large technology companies, Microsoft has significantly increased investments in artificial intelligence infrastructure in recent years. The company is spending heavily on expanding the data centre capacity required to support products such as Copilot and its Azure cloud platform.

The growing energy needs of artificial intelligence systems are creating new challenges for technology companies attempting to meet climate and renewable energy commitments. Some of the data centres currently under development by major technology firms are expected to require multiple gigawatts of electricity capacity. Industry estimates suggest that one gigawatt of power is sufficient to supply electricity to roughly 750,000 homes in the United States.

The surge in electricity demand has triggered increased investment across energy sectors, including renewable energy, battery storage, natural gas and nuclear power. Industry executives have argued that gas-fired power projects can often be deployed faster than renewable energy infrastructure, particularly as demand from data centres rises sharply.

In 2024, Microsoft signed a power agreement with Constellation Energy linked to the planned revival of a unit at the Three Mile Island nuclear plant in Pennsylvania. The developments reflect the growing tension between the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence infrastructure and the climate targets adopted by global technology companies over the past decade.

value.Title: Can the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-0VR3-S169-R4KR-00000-00Can the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:17ZLexisNexisCan the US harness old oil and gas wells to produce geothermal energy?

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Maria Gallucci

May 12th, 2026 ( Canary Media - Delivered by Newstex )

Millions of inactive wells are littered across the United States, the relics of earlier eras of fossil fuel production. A large number of the sites have no official owner, and many are still polluting groundwater and leaking heat-trapping methane. The country has barely scratched the surface in dealing with this problem.

Policymakers in both Republican- and Democratic-led states are exploring whether these sites could instead be converted into new wells for producing geothermal energy. The holes are already drilled in the ground, after all. And regions with widespread oil and gas development have rich subsurface data that geothermal firms need in order to determine where and how to build their carbon-free systems.

The concept is relatively new and largely untested, though scientists and startups are working to change that. States are also laying the groundwork for action by lifting regulatory hurdles and launching in-depth studies.

In Oklahoma, the state Senate is considering a bill that would create a process for companies to buy abandoned oil and gas wells and repurpose them for geothermal energy or underground energy storage. Oklahoma has identified over 20,000 such wells, and state regulators estimate that it would take 235 years and hundreds of millions of dollars to plug all of them. Fixing a single old well can cost anywhere from $75,000 to $150,000 or more, by some calculations, depending on where it's located and how complicated it is to clean up.

The Well Repurposing Act, which passed Oklahoma's House in March, is modeled after a similar law that New Mexico adopted last year to address its 2,000-plus orphan wells.

The Oklahoma bill 'recognizes that these wells are a liability, and that there may be a way to turn them into some sort of revenue generation and give them value,' said Dave Tragethon, communications director for the nonprofit Well Done Foundation, which works to find and cap abandoned oil and gas wells nationwide. 'And if there's value, that means there's more of a willingness to address them and more of an opportunity to raise funding.'

In Alabama, legislators passed a law last month that allows the state to approve and regulate the conversion of oil and gas wells to tap alternative energy resources like geothermal. And in Colorado, state agencies just launched a technical study to evaluate the potential of repurposing old wells for geothermal development and carbon capture and sequestration.

These efforts reflect the growing bipartisan support for geothermal energy, which has largely remained unscathed by the Trump administration's efforts to block renewable energy projects. The energy resource has the potential to help meet the nation's soaring energy demand while also slashing planet-warming emissions from electricity and heating.

Converting wells is enticing but complicated

Geothermal systems work by circulating fluids underground to capture naturally occurring heat, which can then be used to drive turbines for generating electricity or to directly warm the air and water in buildings. The industry is gaining momentum thanks to recent advances in drilling methods and technologies that are making it technically possible or financially viable to access geothermal energy in more places.

Many of those breakthroughs have come from the oil and gas industry, whose skilled workforce of drilling engineers and geoscientists, and deep corporate pockets, have helped launch startups and deploy cutting-edge systems. However, most of that expertise and funding are being poured into building new projects - not figuring out how to retool leaky wells left behind by earlier generations.

'Oil and gas well conversion presents an enormous opportunity, but it's pretty far away technologically from being a reality,' said Emily Pope, a geologist and senior fellow at the Center for Climate and Energy Solutions who authored a recent study on next-generation geothermal power.

'There are some hurdles that are still pretty immense,' she said, adding that 'it is worth doing some R&D to try and grow.'

One of the biggest challenges is the fact that oil and gas wells tend to reach relatively low to medium underground temperatures. But high heat is key for geothermal projects, especially ones that generate electricity. The hotter the resource, the more energy a developer can wring out of the system.

Plus, fossil fuel wells generally produce smaller volumes of liquid and gas than geothermal wells need in order to spin power turbines or transfer heat to buildings. Geothermal operators might also have to take extra steps to keep nasty elements in the subsurface reservoirs from mixing with the working fluids used to extract heat underground, said Arash Dahi Taleghani, an engineering professor with the Repurposing Center for Energy Transition at Pennsylvania State University.

He added that the high cost of converting wells to geothermal has limited the number of real-world examples so far.

Early research efforts target direct-use heat and storage

At the University of Oklahoma, however, researchers have been evaluating how to turn four old oil and gas wells into sources of geothermal heat, which they hope to pipe into nearby public schools and homes in the city of Tuttle. The project was awarded a $1.7 million grant from the U.S. Department of Energy's Wells of Opportunity program in 2022, though it was paused last year during the Trump administration's sweeping freeze on federal funding and is still waiting to start its next phase, KGOU reported in March.

Saeed Salehi was the Oklahoma project's director before joining Southern Methodist University as an engineering professor in 2024. He said that repurposing wells for geothermal has several 'clear advantages.'

Geothermal firms can avoid significant upfront drilling costs if the wells are already sufficiently deep and hot enough. Oil and gas firms, which today pay millions of dollars to properly seal and shut down modern wells, can give their assets a second life instead. And communities near the aging fossil fuel infrastructure could benefit from having clean, affordable heat and lower winter utility bills.

'We need to collect enough data and have enough successful projects to take it to scale,' Salehi said, calling repurposed wells 'a custom solution for specific regions and areas.'

'Everything is going to take time, but I think we are moving in the right direction,' he added.

A smoother permitting process will be key to speeding things up, something Oklahoma, Alabama, and other states are aiming to address. States have traditionally lacked any regulatory framework for dealing with decades-old wells that no one is technically responsible for. Salehi said it took nearly nine months to get the Tuttle project's permits, though the process is growing faster now.

In Pennsylvania, Dahi Taleghani said his team is looking to secure funding to repurpose old wells to supply the Penn State campus with geothermal heating. They have also studied the potential for using some of the state's more than 200,000 abandoned wells to heat agricultural greenhouses, as well as to house energy-storage systems that compress air and stash it underground, acting as low-cost grid batteries.

'Decommissioning wells is expensive, costly, and it's not generating any revenue,' Dahi Taleghani said. 'So we're looking to [help] create businesses that can go after these leaky wells, fix them, and then use them for geothermal applications.'

value.Title: WEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-DVG3-S2B7-830P-00000-00WEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisWEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY [10 Press Releases]

+ PENN STATE SUSTAINABLE LABS PROGRAM COMPLETES FOURTH YEAR, BROADENING CAMPUS REACH AND CUTTING LAB EMISSIONS

The Penn State Sustainable Labs Program has finished its fourth year, expanding its reach across campuses while reducing the environmental footprint of research spaces. The 2025-26 cohort included 20 labs from multiple colleges, with seven labs recertifying from the inaugural 2022-23 cohort. Participation remains free for all labs."The opportunity to cut impact without hindering discovery is urgent and practical," says Jack Rumery.

+ PSU IST PROFESSOR EARNS CAIS FELLOWSHIP TO EXAMINE GLOBAL MISINFORMATION AND FACT-CHECKING

Dongwon Lee, a professor in Penn State's College of Information Sciences and Technology, has been awarded a fellowship at the Center for Advanced Internet Studies (CAIS) in Bochum, Germany. He will study how misinformation spreads on a global scale and how artificial intelligence can help identify false information online more quickly and easily. CAIS, which funds research on the societal opportunities and challenges of digital transformation, will host Lee from July through September. He serves as interim head of IST's Department of Privacy and Cybersecurity Informatics and is a faculty affiliate at Penn State's Center for Socially Responsible Artificial Intelligence. 'To maintain a trustworthy and healthy digital ecosystem, it is critically important to improve our understanding of global patterns of fact-checking, especially under the growing influence of generative AI and AI hallucinations,' Lee said. 'Through the new collaborations I hope to establish in Germany and across Europe, I aim to gain access to new European fact-checking datasets and develop novel research questions.' Lee will use a collection of about 200,000 fact-checked claims from around the world, written in 39 languages, to study how false or misleading information spreads and how people try to verify it. He aims to answer two main questions.

+ PENN STATE MECHANICAL ENGINEERING AWARDS BRYANT EARLY CAREER PROFESSORSHIP TO REID BERDANIER

Reid Berdanier, associate professor of mechanical engineering, has earned the Bryant Early Career Professorship. The three-year award runs through 2028 and makes him the eighth current faculty member to hold a named early-career professorship. Mary Frecker, head of the Department of Mechanical Engineering, notes that recipients work in healthcare, transportation, and renewable energy storage.

+ PENN STATE, FAO MARK ONE-YEAR ANNIVERSARY OF YOUTH FOOD LAB NORTH AMERICA PARTNERSHIP

Penn State's College of Agricultural Sciences welcomed a delegation from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations to University Park last week to mark the first anniversary of the World Food Forum Youth Food Lab North America at Penn State partnership and to explore opportunities to deepen collaboration on global agrifood systems, innovation and education. Deanna Behring, assistant dean and director of Ag Sciences Global, described the partnership as expanding a long-standing engagement in food security research, teaching and outreach. "As a land-grant university, our college has had a long-standing mission - dating back to 1961 and the U.S. Foreign Assistance Act that called upon universities to build partnerships in the name of food security and the development of agriculture as an underpinning to healthy and equitable communities and economies globally," she said. "This new step in our partnership with FAO builds on our previous engagement by bringing new energy and innovation to areas critical to both our missions."

+ WE ARE! HONORS 22 PENN STATERS FOR EXTRAORDINARY SERVICE — MAY 11

As part of our regular \"We Are!\" feature, we recognize 22 Penn Staters who have gone above and beyond what's asked of them in their work at the University. This week, on behalf of the entire Penn State community, we'd like to say thank you to four outstanding colleagues: Sarah Davis, educator for food, families, and communities in Penn State Extension; Sarah DeWolfe, assistant director of financial coaching in the Sokolov-Miller Family Financial and Life Skills Center; Michele Duffey, associate teaching professor of kinesiology in the College of Health and Human Development; and Lisa Edwards, academic adviser in Smeal College of Business. Each of these honorees demonstrates leadership and collaboration that help students and colleagues succeed. Davis provides program leadership, listens to concerns, explains challenges, and seeks improvements within required systems. DeWolfe steps into interim roles when leaves occur and consistently supports her teammates. Duffey engages across the University to hear diverse voices and keep students' best interests at heart. Edwards offers a welcoming presence, a positive attitude, and compassionate care that helps students thrive. These efforts reflect the Penn State community’s spirit of service, and we thank these four honorees for exemplifying the We Are! commitment.

+ PENN STATE AND CLIMAVISION FORGE PARTNERSHIP TO TRAIN FUTURE METEOROLOGISTS

A new partnership between Penn State and Climavision aims to expand research and training opportunities for students and faculty and could strengthen weather detection and warnings across the commonwealth. Climavision will provide access to its supplemental network of high-resolution weather radars, designed to cover areas not well-sampled by the National Weather Service. The data will be available in real time and historically to support classroom, research and applied learning. Climavision's scientists will also offer lectures and training for the Penn State community. "The Climavision radar data will potentially be a treasure trove for our students with opportunities afforded for both research and classroom activities," Markowski said. Climavision's data supports analyses of severe weather, precipitation, lake-effect snow, smoke plumes and other atmospheric conditions, and the company's expanding network is intended to bolster low-level observations where public systems have limited coverage, a gap affecting about 4 million people in Pennsylvania.

+ PENN STATE RESEARCHERS REPORT MIXED RESULTS FROM PRESCRIBED FIRE ON INVASIVE BURNING BUSH AND BORDER PRIVET

Invasive woody plants, including burning bush and border privet, threaten eastern U.S. forests by crowding natives. Penn State researchers tested three burning regimens and reported mixed results for these aggressive invaders. The team published their findings in Ecosphere.

+ PENN STATE Q&A: PRACTICAL STEPS TO GUARD AGAINST TICKS

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa. - May is Lyme Disease Awareness Month, and Pennsylvania is among the top states for Lyme and other tick-borne illnesses. Experts say there are steps to protect people, pets and livestock from ticks and the pathogens they carry. Erika Machtinger, associate professor of entomology in Penn State's College of Agricultural Sciences, studies ticks and other arthropod pests and the risks they pose to humans and animals. She notes there are more than 900 tick species worldwide, with at least 25 found in Pennsylvania. Of those, two species account for more than 90% of identification requests submitted to Penn State: the blacklegged tick and the American dog tick. In Pennsylvania, three additional species - the lone star tick, Asian longhorn tick and winter tick - are also present.

+ PENN STATE Q&A: IS AI DEMOCRATIZING GLOBAL HEALTH OR REINFORCING OLD INEQUITIES?

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa.—AI is changing the tools at the core of global health policy, from disease-control modeling to financing and vaccination planning. Matt Ferrari, a Penn State biology professor and director of the Center for Infectious Disease Dynamics, says these advances create new modeling possibilities but could widen disparities if not guided thoughtfully."There's an irrational optimism that AI, machine learning and computational tools are going to democratize access to science and policy influence," Ferrari said. "AI, if treated naively, will likely reinforce existing inequities so we should be proactive about avoiding that."

+ PENN STATE CHEM-E-CAR EARNS REGIONAL VICTORY, ADVANCES TO GLOBAL COMPETITION

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa. - Penn State's Chem-E-Car team, part of the AIChE student chapter, finished first out of 21 schools in the Chem-E-Car Performance Competition at the 2026 AIChE Mid-Atlantic Conference, earning a berth at the 2026 Annual Student Conference, an international event scheduled for November. Last year, the Penn State squad placed fourth in the global competition."From the time I joined to now, we have all learned a lot, worked hard and made many improvements to our approach to developing each car," said Meilisa Thomas, a fourth-year chemical engineering major and the club's president. "I see these recent successes as an indication that we're heading in the right direction."

value.Title: Report: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5B

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-CMP3-RYY8-G4P1-00000-00Report: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5B2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisNEWSReport: Carbon taxes cost Pennsylvania $5BChristen Smith The Center Square

(The Center Square) – Energy is big business in Pennsylvania.

Its storied fossil fuel reserves have kept utility bills lower and the regional power grid more stable for the better part of a century.

That’s remained key as states opt for lower-emission power sources, which have so far relied on fledgling technologies not yet able to keep the lights on with batteries, solar panels and wind turbines alone.

A new report from the Commonwealth Foundation concludes it could have been even bigger business had state regulators not spent the last six years “flirting” with a carbon tax program called the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, the success of which many critics say relies on the fossil fuels it means to curb.

For the program’s most ardent supporters, the transition away from coal and gas must start somewhere. And, to them, a 50% reduction in emissions and $6 billion in revenue over the last 18 years isn’t a bad one.

“The Keystone State’s six-year on-again, off-again relationship with RGGI is a cautionary tale for other states, such as Virginia,” said Elizabeth Stelle, vice president of policy for the Commonwealth Foundation. “RGGI is a carbon tax in sheep’s clothing, toppling the energy future of every state it touches.”

RGGI, as the program is often referred to, profits from reductions in harmful emissions by charging generators for the air pollution they create, measured in carbon dioxide. Across 12 states in the mid-Atlantic and New England, power plants and industrial sites buy an ever-decreasing number of emissions “credits” at the program’s annual auction to cover that anticipated pollution.

Those credit prices have risen sharply since RGGI’s first auction in 2008, and because of the balance needed between fossil fuels and renewable power, the program has put financial pressure on the entire grid, leaving utility companies – and by extension, ratepayers – scrambling to make up the difference.

Sometimes relief comes from RGGI-funded bill-reduction programs administered by participating states. Or the proliferation of more efficient renewable sources, supported by government taxpayer-funded subsidies, that require less support from gas and coal. In recent years, it’s been the pooling of smart thermostats, rooftop solar panels, and battery storage to compensate consumers for lower electricity usage.

And yes, it’s been rate increases too. But if not for Pennsylvania, where 25% of the regional power grid’s electricity is sourced, conventional wisdom has long held that those hikes could have been far steeper.

Which is why former two-term Democratic Gov. Tom Wolf’s 2019 executive order adding Pennsylvania to the RGGI program triggered a six-year regulatory war with lawmakers to stop it before it ever happened.

The foundation, a conservative policy group based in Harrisburg, was among the loudest critics – behind legislative Republicans, with whom the organization typically aligns – of the proposal, citing quadrupled energy costs and a $5 billion investment shortfall, according to its report.

In terms of raw numbers, the chilling effect of potentially joining RGGI is stark. Between 2013 and 2018, 73% of proposed power projects in the commonwealth became operational. After regulators set their sights on RGGI in 2019, that conversion rate collapsed to 9%.

By comparison, the rate for Ohio – a non-RGGI state with a wealth of fossil fuel plants – declined much more slowly, from 62% to 48%, in part due to falling gas prices.

“Unfortunately, that loss is not shocking,” Stelle said. “While Pennsylvania debated, litigated, and delayed over carbon tax policy, Ohio provided developers with a predictable regulatory environment to commit billions in capital. Projects that might have started in Pennsylvania went to Ohio or never came to fruition.”

Losing to “friggin’ Ohio” has jokingly been a thorn in the side of first-term Democratic Gov. Josh Shapiro since he took office in 2023, even calling out the Buckeye State’s competitive economic policies during his first budget address to the General Assembly.

Shapiro, on the campaign trail, expressed his doubts about RGGI, though he continued the Wolf administration’s legal fight to make Pennsylvania participate for two more years, only dropping it as part of a four-month-late budget deal this past November.

RGGI detractors say taxing generators would not just make it less profitable to build new power plants. It would raise energy costs for everyone by accelerating the closure of long-serving facilities, reducing supply, and raising electricity prices.

Shapiro, too, worries about affordability and instability, though he’s pointed the finger at PJM, the organization that manages the power grid, and at its lengthy planning process. He even filed complaints with federal regulators to force the organization to lower electricity price caps, temporarily preventing a 30% spike for ratepayers.

He’s even threatened, more than once, to decouple the state from the grid and “go it alone” – an unprecedented move that could undo a century of cooperation.

It hasn’t come to that yet. Instead, Shapiro has lobbied the General Assembly to act on his state-centric Lightning Plan to quadruple wind and solar power over the decade – and still limit emissions from larger plants.

Stelle said doing so would double residential electricity bills.

“Affordable, reliable energy is not achieved through Green New Deal-style mandates and burdensome regulation,” she said. “It happens naturally when we pursue policies that welcome the investment its grid desperately needs, letting Pennsylvania’s rich energy sector do what it does best.”

In the eyes of some, air pollution is what the energy industry does best. In 2020, federal data rankedPennsylvania fourth nationwide for carbon emissions, and estimates suggest that 1% of global greenhouse gas pollution comes from the commonwealth alone.

Supporters of programs like RGGI often point to the dual impacts of reduced emissions and investment in renewable technology as key to their value for an energy-rich state like Pennsylvania.

A 2023 report from the Kleinman Center for Energy Policy at the University of Pennsylvania concluded that RGGI participation would have reduced the state’s electricity-sector emissions by 84% in 2030, compared to roughly 52% without it.

It could have been achieved without raising utility bills or decimating energy exports, according to the report. Researchers said wind and solar generation would have softened the impact of lost natural gas production and retail electricity prices will actually decline 0.6% by the time the RGGI cap zeroes out in 2040.

Shapiro’s state-based plans – the Pennsylvania Reliable Energy Sustainability Standard and the Pennsylvania Climate Emissions Reduction Act, PRESS and PACER for short – similarly broaden the mix of energy providers so that the power grid isn’t sustained primarily by fossil fuels.

This time, however, 70% of the money recovered from state-imposed emissions caps would support bill-reduction programs.

And, based on modeling from Synapse Energy Economics that was commissioned by the state Department of Environmental Protection last year, it would lower residential bills by $1 per month.

What’s not entirely clear is how rapid data center development would collide with such policy, if it were to be implemented. More than 50 proposals are pending across the state, and their economic viability could falter if the state recommits to carbon taxing programs.

Steam plumes rise from a geothermal power plant. Photo by Sam Bark/Unsplash

value.Title: Siemens Energy May Gain Supply-Chain Edge Following GE Vernova's ORIDEN, LLC v. GE PROLEC TRANSFORMERS, INC. Pennsylvania Contract Challenge *Updated*

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-8BN3-S333-S1RM-00000-00Siemens Energy May Gain Supply-Chain Edge Following GE Vernova's ORIDEN, LLC v. GE PROLEC TRANSFORMERS, INC. Pennsylvania Contract Challenge *Updated*2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisSiemens Energy May Gain Supply-Chain Edge Following GE Vernova's ORIDEN, LLC v. GE PROLEC TRANSFORMERS, INC. Pennsylvania Contract Challenge *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

What Stakeholders Need To Know

This situation demonstrates how supply agreement obligations in the energy infrastructure and power-transformer markets may lead to state-level breach of contract claims that are subsequently removed to federal jurisdictions. Corporations encounter potential oversight requirements when subsidiaries or joint ventures are named as defendants and parent companies are listed in legal disclosures. The resolution of such matters may result in industry-wide evaluations of contract stipulations, parent-liability terms, and risk-allocation frameworks, potentially increasing compliance expenditures and altering procurement procedures. Additional industry effects might involve stricter standards for vendor management and corporate transparency in transformer supply chains for grid projects, which could impact operational processes across the power equipment sector.

Background of the Case

The legal proceeding, ORIDEN, LLC v. GE PROLEC TRANSFORMERS, INC., No. 2:26-cv-00554 (W.D. Pa.), was transferred to federal court on April 2, 2026, originating from the Pennsylvania Court of Common Pleas, Allegheny County (GD-26-002212, filed on February 26, 2026). The plaintiff, ORIDEN, LLC, claims a breach of contract. The defendant, GE PROLEC TRANSFORMERS, INC., submitted a Disclosure Statement naming GE Vernova, Inc. as its corporate parent. The nature of the suit is classified under 190 Contract - Other Contract, with the cause listed as 28:1441 Notice of Removal - Breach of Contract. Judge David S. Cercone is presiding. The defendant submitted a Motion to Dismiss on April 9, 2026, with the briefing schedule expected to finish in May 2026. The proceedings are currently in the initial stages following the removal.

Competitive Impacts on Siemens Energy

Siemens Energy's power-transformer and grid segments might experience indirect operational advantages if the market perceives increased contract reviews at GE Vernova and Prolec facilities. Industry observations indicate that renewable energy developers are carefully assessing potential partners to ensure straightforward supply agreements. This market condition could result in a gradual realignment of transformer procurement choices favoring Siemens Energy's operations in the United States and Europe.

Possible Opportunities for Siemens Energy

Increased acquisition of transformer supply contracts in situations where contract dependability serves as a primary evaluation factor.

Improved standing in joint-venture proposals that require explicit risk-allocation parameters.

Broadened associations with developers involved in North American energy infrastructure initiatives.

Reinforced corporate reputation based on documented supply-chain governance standards.

Why This Case Is Challenging for Siemens Energy

Despite having no direct participation, the existence of an active breach-of-contract dispute involving parent company disclosure might initiate broader regulatory or investor examinations of industry supply agreements. This situation could necessitate that Siemens Energy assess its internal contract frameworks and possibly undertake supplementary compliance audits.

Key Challenges for Siemens Energy

Industry-wide increases in contract-liability assessments and expenditures related to policy standardization.

Additional inquiries from analysts and investors concerning supply-chain management structures.

Possible peripheral litigation exposure should comparable circumstances arise within Siemens Energy contracts.

Increased requirements for legal reserves connected to commercial contracting procedures in United States energy developments.

Competitor's Strategic Responses

Executed internal evaluations comparing supply agreement templates with current federal removal criteria.

Revised procurement systems to incorporate specific language focused on contract compliance.

Initiated directed communications with renewable energy developers to emphasize contract stability.

Retained outside legal counsel to conduct preventative compliance assessments throughout North American divisions.

Key Measures

Siemens Energy is executing these strategies via cross-functional teams that report to senior operational management, facilitating systematic incorporation into global supply processes while upholding established project schedules.

Possible Losses and Unexpected Advantages

Potential Advantages

Expansion of market share in transformer agreements, potentially resulting in supplementary revenue projections reaching $210 million.

Enhanced developer assessments that improve competitive positioning for infrastructure bids.

Expedited project initiation within highly competitive supply sectors.

Fortified environmental, social, and governance ratings resulting from established contract administration frameworks.

Potential Losses

Aggregate expenses for compliance and document revisions projected at approximately $105 million throughout global networks.

Transitional reallocation of administrative personnel away from primary technological programs.

Increased legal expenditures associated with comprehensive contract evaluations.

Near-term profit margin constraints resulting from expedited investments in procurement infrastructure.

Final Thoughts

Siemens Energy has the capacity to utilize the present industry conditions by verifying its standing as a dependable power equipment supplier while strengthening internal contract parameters concerning wider disclosure requirements. A calculated methodology that aligns commercial opportunities with rigorous compliance protocols enables the organization to manage industry modifications in supply criteria, thereby sustaining its position within energy infrastructure markets.

Tags: Pennsylvania contract suit involving GE Prolec identified GE Vernova as corporate parent

Activities

GE Vernova, a spin-off from General Electric, is a global leader in the energy sector, focusing on innovative solutions for power generation, renewable energy, and electrification. The company's core activities encompass the design, manufacturing, and servicing of technologies for gas and steam power plants, wind turbines, hydroelectric systems, and grid management solutions, enabling reliable and efficient energy delivery worldwide. Its fastest-growing segments include offshore wind and electrification technologies, driven by rising demand for sustainable energy sources amid global decarbonization efforts. Additionally, GE Vernova leverages advanced digital platforms for predictive maintenance and asset optimization, enhancing operational efficiency for utilities and industrial clients. With a strong presence in markets like the U.S. NYSE, the company is positioned to capitalize on the transition to cleaner energy, supporting infrastructure development and energy security. It is the NYSE's largest Energy company by market capitalisation.

GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) is poised as a leading force in the renewable energy sector, leveraging its expertise as a premier wind turbine manufacturer to drive sustainable progress globally. Central to its operations are the GE Vernova wind turbines, including advanced models like the GE Vernova Haliade-X 14MW and the GE Vernova Cypress onshore turbine. Strategic projects, such as the GE Vernova wind turbine supply Spain and the GE Vernova Spain wind project, underscore its robust market presence and capability in executing large-scale GE Vernova wind farm supply contracts. By focusing on innovative solutions like GE Vernova floating offshore turbines, GE Vernova grid connection solutions, and comprehensive GE Vernova O&M services, the company ensures superior turbine performance and efficiency. With a commitment to excellence, evidenced by the GE Vernova wind turbine maintenance and GE Vernova turbine commissioning, GE Vernova is strategically positioned for long-term growth, especially in green energy in Spain.

value.Title: WEEKLY INDUSTRY OVERVIEW: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-X4N3-S254-Y41S-00000-00WEEKLY INDUSTRY OVERVIEW: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisWEEKLY INDUSTRY OVERVIEW: THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ THE PENNSYLVANIA STATE UNIVERSITY [10 Press Releases]

+ PENN STATE'S SUSTAINABLE LABS PROGRAM WRAPS FOURTH YEAR, HIGHLIGHTING CARBON SAVINGS AND STUDENT ENGAGEMENT

The Penn State Sustainable Labs Program has completed its fourth year, expanding its reach across campuses while advancing the drive to reduce the environmental footprint of research spaces. The 2025-26 cohort included 20 labs across multiple colleges, with seven labs recertifying after the initial 2022-23 run. Participation remains free, and the program emphasizes student engagement through its Sustainable Lab Consultant model, pairing students with labs to implement sustainable practices. The year also saw My Green Lab 2.0, a more responsive platform with expanded tools to track progress; labs reported saving over $100,000 and reducing about 500 metric tons of CO2e through behavior-based changes.

+ IST FACULTY MEMBER AT PENN STATE RECEIVES FELLOWSHIP TO STUDY MISINFORMATION AND FACT-CHECKING PRACTICES WORLDWIDE

Dongwon Lee, a professor at Penn State's College of Information Sciences and Technology, has received a CAIS fellowship in Bochum, Germany, to study misinformation globally and the use of artificial intelligence to speed up spotting false information online. CAIS funds research on the societal opportunities and challenges of digital transformation and will host Lee from July through September. He is interim head of IST's Department of Privacy and Cybersecurity Informatics and a faculty affiliate at Penn State's Center for Socially Responsible Artificial Intelligence. He noted that collaborations in Germany and across Europe could provide access to European fact-checking datasets and help generate new research questions. Lee will use a collection of about 200,000 fact-checked claims from around the world, written in 39 languages, to study how false information spreads and how people verify it. He aims to answer two main questions.

+ EIGHT CURRENT FACULTY HOLD NAMED EARLY-CAREER PROFESSORSHIPS IN PENN STATE MECHANICAL ENGINEERING

Reid Berdanier, associate professor of mechanical engineering, has been awarded the Bryant Early Career Professorship. The three-year appointment runs through 2028, and he is the department’s eighth current holder of a named early-career professorship. Mary Frecker notes that recipients work across healthcare, transportation systems, and renewable energy storage.

+ PENN STATE'S COLLEGE OF AGRICULTURAL SCIENCES DEEPENS TIES WITH FAO ON YOUTH FOOD LAB MILESTONE

Penn State's College of Agricultural Sciences welcomed a delegation from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations to mark the one-year anniversary of the World Food Forum Youth Food Lab North America at Penn State partnership and to explore deeper collaboration on global agrifood systems, innovation and education. Deanna Behring, assistant dean and director of Ag Sciences Global, noted that the partnership builds on a long-standing mission dating back to 1961 and the U.S. Foreign Assistance Act, aimed at advancing food security and agricultural development globally. "This new step in our partnership with FAO builds on our previous engagement by bringing new energy and innovation to areas critical to both our missions." The delegation's visit included discussions with Penn State leaders and students, including FAO deputy director-general Beth Bechdol, Lauren Phillips, director of partnerships and UN collaboration, and Jocelyn Brown Hall, director of the Liaison Office for North America, about global agrifood systems and workforce development.

+ WE ARE! SPOTLIGHT CELEBRATES 22 CAMPUS CONTRIBUTORS — MAY 11

In its weekly We Are! feature, Penn State recognizes 22 staff members who have gone above and beyond in service to the University. This week we highlight four colleagues whose peers note their leadership and teamwork: Sarah Davis, educator for food, families, and communities in Penn State Extension, is praised for delivering strong programs and providing leadership, with attentive listening, clear communication, and a focus on improving internal processes. Sarah DeWolfe, assistant director of financial coaching in the Sokolov-Miller Family Financial and Life Skills Center, is described as reliable, knowledgeable, and collaborative, stepping into interim roles when needed and supporting the team. Michele Duffey, associate teaching professor of kinesiology in the College of Health and Human Development, is celebrated for listening to diverse voices, solving problems, and advancing student success through cross-campus collaboration. Lisa Edwards, academic adviser in Smeal College of Business, is recognized for creating a welcoming office atmosphere, maintaining a positive attitude, and delivering compassionate care to students. Their efforts reflect the Penn State commitment to service and teamwork that defines the We Are! tradition.

+ NEW ALLIANCE BETWEEN PENN STATE AND CLIMAVISION AIMS TO BOOST METEOROLOGY TRAINING AND WEATHER DETECTION

Penn State has launched a collaboration with Climavision to advance meteorology research and education by giving the university access to a broader radar data toolkit and technical support. Climavision will provide access to its supplemental network of high-resolution weather radars to complement public coverage and support both classroom and research work, with data available in real time and historically. Climavision scientists will contribute lectures and training to the Penn State community, while researchers examine applications such as severe weather, precipitation patterns, lake-effect snow and smoke plumes. "The Climavision radar data will potentially be a treasure trove for our students with opportunities afforded for both research and classroom activities," Markowski said. Climavision's data covers myriad weather phenomena and is aimed at boosting low-level observations in areas where public systems have limited coverage, including roughly 4 million people in Pennsylvania.

+ STUDY FROM PENN STATE DOCUMENTS PARTIAL SUCCESS OF PRESCRIBED-FIRE REGIMENS AGAINST BURNING BUSH AND BORDER PRIVET

Invasive woody plants, including burning bush and border privet, pose a challenge in eastern U.S. forests by crowding out native species. Researchers at Penn State tested three burning regimens on these invaders and reported mixed results. The work, published in Ecosphere, highlights that prescribed fire may help modestly in some settings but is not consistently effective. The study underscores the complexity of fire management as a strategy to restore native plant communities.

+ LYME DISEASE AWARENESS MONTH: PENN STATE EXPLAINS TICK SPECIES IN PENNSYLVANIA AND PREVENTION

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa. - May is Lyme Disease Awareness Month, and Pennsylvania is among the states with the highest reported cases of Lyme disease and other tick-borne illnesses. Penn State says there are steps to protect people, pets and livestock from ticks and the pathogens they carry. Erika Machtinger, associate professor of entomology in Penn State's College of Agricultural Sciences, is a specialist in ticks and other arthropod pests and the risks they pose to humans and animals. There are more than 900 tick species worldwide, with at least 25 believed to exist in Pennsylvania. Of these, the blacklegged tick and the American dog tick account for the vast majority of identification requests to Penn State. Pennsylvania is home to several other species as well, including the lone star tick, Asian longhorn tick and winter tick.

+ CAN AI EQUALIZE GLOBAL HEALTH DECISION-MAKING OR ENTRENCH INEQUALITY? A PENN STATE PERSPECTIVE

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa.— AI is reshaping global health decision tools, from disease modeling to financing and vaccination planning, according to Matt Ferrari, a Penn State biology professor and director of the Center for Infectious Disease Dynamics. He says the changes bring opportunities for more informed policy, but also carry the risk of widening existing gaps if not managed carefully."There's an irrational optimism that AI, machine learning and computational tools are going to democratize access to science and policy influence," Ferrari said. "AI, if treated naively, will likely reinforce existing inequities so we should be proactive about avoiding that."The key, he adds, is to design AI systems with equity in mind, ensuring they are transparent and accessible to a broad range of users.

+ CHEM-E-CAR PROGRAM AT PENN STATE EARNS REGIONAL HONOR, EARNS SPOT IN GLOBAL EVENT

UNIVERSITY PARK, Pa. - Penn State's Chem-E-Car team, part of the AIChE student chapter, placed first among 21 teams at the 2026 AIChE Mid-Atlantic Conference, earning a spot at the 2026 Annual Student Conference, an international competition slated for November. The win demonstrates ongoing progress by the program, which is building toward the global finals through regional competition and sustained student effort. Meilisa Thomas, the club president, praised the team's growth, saying, "From the time I joined to now, we have all learned a lot, worked hard and made many improvements to our approach to developing each car," and adding that the success shows they are heading in the right direction.

value.Title: St. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bank South Florida's news briefs for the week of May 8
value.Overview: South Florida Business Journal (Fort Lauderdale/Miami, Florida), 552words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-9473-RYTS-72V1-00000-00St. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bank2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisSt. Regis Residences secures $114 million loan from local bankSouth Florida's news briefs for the week of May 8Eileen Cukier

Owners Lombardi Properties and Fox Farm Capital announced the completion of The Doris, an eight-story boutique office property at 153 N.W. 25th St., in Miami. www.lombardiproperties.com.

PMG launched sales for One Twenty Brickell Signature Residences, a 40-story mixed-use tower offering a deeded office suite with each of its 467 fully furnished residences, at 120 S.W. Eighth St. in Miami. www.onetwentyresidences.com.

Blue Oceans Yachting relocated its Fort Lauderdale office to 1635 Miami Road, Suite 3. www.blueoceansyachting.com.

McDowell Housing Partners completed Miami Jewish Health's Douglas Gardens Senior Health and Living, a 410-unit mixed-income, mixed-use residential community at 711 and 713 S.W. 88th Ave. in Pembroke Pines. www.douglasgardensapts.com.

Broward Health broke ground on an eight-story, 188,000-square-foot medical office building and parking garage at Broward Health Medical Center, 1600 S. Andrews Ave. in Fort Lauderdale. www.browardhealth.org.

Miami-based I Squared Capital's Energy Transition Fund acquired Oriden, a Pittsburgh-based renewable energy developer, from Mitsubishi Power Americas. www.isquaredcapital.com.

Ed Morse Automotive Group acquired Graven Chrysler Jeep Ram in Lebanon, Missouri. www.edmorse.com.

Miami-based Consertus acquired Laceco, a Beirut-based architecture, engineering and planning consultancy. www.consertus.com.

Lexington, Kentucky-based managed cybersecurity services provider 13 Layers opened an office at Crown Center Executive Suites, 1451 W. Cypress Creek Road, Suite 300, in Fort Lauderdale. www.13layers.com.

Fortune International Group and Château Group closed on a $113.75 million land loan with City National Bank of Florida for the North Tower at The St. Regis Residences, Sunny Isles Beach. The tower is on track to break ground at 18801 Collins Ave. this summer. www.srresidencessunnyislesbeach.com.

The Lynd Group secured $34.5 million in refinancing from A10 Capital for Parc Place Apartments, a multifamily community at 17600 N.W. Fifth Ave. in Miami Gardens. www.lynd.com.

Berkadia secured a $21.58 million loan on behalf of Rental Asset Management to refinance South Pointe Apartments, a 170-unit apartment community at 17800 S.W. 107th Ave. in the West Perrine area of Miami-Dade County. www.berkadia.com.

Florida Community Loan Fund closed on $11 million in New Markets Tax Credit financing for Boys & Girls Clubs of Palm Beach County's Julie & Mike Connors Club, a new 31,000-square-foot youth development and workforce readiness clubhouse on 1.65 acres at 1150 Southern Blvd. in West Palm Beach. www.fclf.org.

Berger Commercial Realty facilitated Sand Lake Realty's $6.325 million purchase of the small-bay industrial property at 1241-1251 N Dixie Highway in Pompano Beach, which it will rebrand as Airpark Commerce Center. www.bergercommercial.com.

To be considered for inclusion, please email news releases and high-resolution photos to newstipssfbj@bizjournals.com

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1: BIOGRAPHY: CHARLES E. YOUNG, CEO AND DIRECTOR

SECTION 2: SMART SAND: SEC FILINGS

SECTION 1: BIOGRAPHY: CHARLES E. YOUNG, CEO AND DIRECTOR

Charles E. Young was named Chief Executive Officer in July 2014. Mr. Young has also served as a director since September 2011. Mr. Young founded Smart Sand, LLC (our predecessor) and served as its President from November 2009 to August 2011. Mr. Young served as our President and Secretary from September 2011 to July 2014. Mr. Young has over 20 years of executive and entrepreneurial experience in the high-technology, telecommunications and renewable energy industries. He previously served as the President and Founder of Premier Building Systems, a construction, solar, geothermal and energy audit company in Pennsylvania and New Jersey from 2006 to 2011. Mr. Young received a B.A. in Political Science from Miami University. Mr. Young is the brother of William John Young, our Executive Vice President of Sales and Logistics. We believe that Mr. Young's industry experience and deep knowledge of our business makes him well suited to serve as Chief Executive Officer and Director.

value.Title: PennEnvironment: Data - PA Ranks Abysmal 47th in U.S. for Renewable Energy Growth

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-K2V3-S1DW-M2TF-00000-00PennEnvironment: Data - PA Ranks Abysmal 47th in U.S. for Renewable Energy Growth2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisPennEnvironment: Data - PA Ranks Abysmal 47th in U.S. for Renewable Energy GrowthTargeted News ServicePHILADELPHIA, Pennsylvania

PennEnvironment issued the following news release:

* * *

NEW DATA: PA ranks abysmal 47th in U.S. for renewable energy growth

State makes small gains after ranking 49th in 2025, but still fails to tap clean energy potential

*

Pennsylvania ranks 47th in the nation for rate of growth in total solar, wind and geothermal generation over the past decade, according to The State of Renewable Energy online interactive dashboard released on Wednesday by the PennEnvironment.

The dashboard shows the growth of six key clean energy technologies across the United States over the past decade, ranking Pennsylvania and other states for solar power, wind power, battery storage, energy efficiency, electric vehicles, and electric vehicle charging stations.

Renewable energy generation over the past decade in the Commonwealth has fallen well behind the national pace, only increasing by 81% compared to the national average increase of nearly 200%.

"Pennsylvanians want and need a cleaner, healthier future, powered by abundant renewable resources," said Belle Sherwood, Climate and Clean Energy advocate with PennEnvironment. "Clearly Pennsylvania isn't doing enough to tap into the benefits of clean energy at the rate of most other states."

The percentage of electricity Pennsylvania gets from renewable sources has risen to only 5%, up from a stagnant 3% since 2016. Pennsylvania is still well behind similar energy states such as Texas, which gets 37% of its electricity from wind and solar.

"In the House Energy Committee, we are looking at every possible option for how to increase Pennsylvania's energy generation so we can protect everyday households from rising energy costs," said Rep. Elizabeth Fiedler. "Renewable energy is the clear answer, with solar and wind being the cheapest and fastest to build. Renewables offer us a real way forward, a more stabilized grid, and a more resilient energy sector."

When it comes to electric vehicles (EVs), Pennsylvania now ranks 11th in percent increase in EV charging ports from 2016-2025. Thanks to federal funding and the state's own investment in EV charging infrastructure, Pennsylvania added over 1,000 more EV charging ports since 2023.

"On one hand, we're seeing rising electricity costs, aging infrastructure, and growing demand placing real pressure on our energy system," said Will Hoover, McClure Company. "On the other hand, we have an enormous opportunity, because solar energy is more accessible, more affordable, and more impactful than ever before."

To move the state from laggard to leader, PennEnvironment is calling on the General Assembly to pass legislation requiring utilities to get at least 35% of their power from renewable energy sources, pass new energy efficiency standards for appliances, and update the state's energy conservation law.

"Investing in renewable energy and energy efficiency will allow nature to thrive and all of us to live healthier and more enriching lives," said Sherwood. "This report is a reminder that although Pennsylvania has made some progress over the past decade, there is much more work to do to power our state with truly clean energy."

* * *

PennEnvironment works for clean air, clean water, clean energy, wildlife and open spaces, and a livable climate. Our members across the state put grassroots support behind our research and advocacy. For more information about our work, visit www.PennEnvironment.org. PennEnvironment is part of Environment America, a national network of 29 state environmental groups.
