value.Title: May 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-G8H3-S3TD-1210-00000-00May 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 11, 2026: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO., INC. PEER LANDSCAPE: RECENT NEWS HIGHLIGHTS

On February 25, 2026, Consigli Construction Co., Inc. commenced work on the Williams College Museum of Art, marking the first purpose-built facility for the museum on the campus. Abigail Berkebile, a prominent figure at the firm, noted the unique design challenges and the rewarding nature of witnessing staff enthusiasm as the building progresses. She also emphasized the diverse career opportunities available for women in the construction industry, noting that teams rely on expertise in accounting, IT, legal, and other fields that do not require a formal construction background. To maintain focus during complex projects, Berkebile highlighted her routine of organizing schedules and connecting with her team daily to foster collaboration.

In the broader competitive landscape, Suffolk Construction is advancing its regional infrastructure commitments. The firm managed the Southeastern Public Service Authority interchange project that led to the Virginia Department of Transportation reopening a long-term lane closure on U.S. Route 58 on May 5, 2026. The completion of this project facilitates a new ramp and flyover for eastbound traffic to access the regional landfill without crossing westbound lanes. Meanwhile, a solar farm in Suffolk currently under construction by Egg Power, set to commence generation in 2027, became the center of a 10-year Power Purchase Agreement announced on May 1, 2026. Virgin Media O2 will source approximately five percent of its total energy demands from this 70MW facility. Mark Hardman, Director of Finance Operations at VMO2, stated that the agreement represents the latest step in securing long-term renewable energy and achieving net-zero emissions by the end of 2040.

Expanding on significant industry developments, Turner Construction celebrated the topping out of the 72-megawatt Aligned IAD-06 Data Center in Frederick, Maryland, on April 28, 2026. The 450,000-square-foot facility, located on a 75-acre campus, logged over 80,000 craft hours with approximately 345 skilled workers. Nick Sevier, Operations Manager at Turner, remarked that the milestone reflects the strength of the partnership with Aligned and the dedication to delivering a complex data center safely. On that same day, Turner Construction commenced vertical construction on a $900 million expansion of the Geisinger Wyoming Valley Medical Center in Pennsylvania. The 11-story, 600,000-square-foot patient tower will increase the hospital's capacity to 500 beds and is slated to open in early 2028. David Kaminski, Vice President and Senior General Manager at Turner, stated that the project will enhance patient care and support caregivers while generating a significant local economic impact.

Shaping its financial strategies to remain competitive with peers, Gilbane Development appointed Andreas Vlahakis as head of capital markets on April 28, 2026. Operating from New York City and reporting to President and CEO James Patchett, Vlahakis oversees funding strategies, investment decisions, and equity partnerships for ground-up multifamily and student housing investments. In a related push for student housing development, the Western Kentucky University Student Government Association confirmed a public-private partnership with Gilbane following legislative approval on April 6, 2026, and initial authorization on December 11, 2025. The firm will design and develop new residence halls to replace outdated facilities on campus. SGA Senator Jakob Barker confirmed the authorization, noting that the university is now cleared to begin building the new accommodations.

value.Title: Campaigners blast solar farm "gold rush" taking place around Cornish village

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-YS93-RVBR-N3CR-00000-00Campaigners blast solar farm "gold rush" taking place around Cornish village2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisCampaigners blast solar farm "gold rush" taking place around Cornish villageLee Trewhela

AN application to build what would be Cornwall's largest solar farm is due to be considered by councillors.

Energy company EDF Renewables has applied to install a huge solar array covering around 82.5 hectares (almost 204 acres) across 19 agricultural fields at Trelion, St Stephen, near St Austell.

A Cornwall Council strategic planning committee has been advised to approve the application at its meeting on Thursday, May 21, despite the local member, St Stephen-in-Brannel Parish Council and over 130 people opposing the proposal.

Cornwall Council's planning department says the weight attached to the provision of renewable energy is significant with the government's National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) now supporting renewable energy provision with fewer restrictions.

A recent planning committee was warned by officers that a decision to refuse another solar application at Marazion would likely be overturned at appeal. As a result, members approved it despite opposition and their own misgivings.

What is currently due to be Cornwall's largest solar farm, on 80 hectares of agricultural land between Mitchell, Trispen, St Erme and Carland Cross, was approved on appeal. The Fair Park solar farm in the Hendra valley was initially refused by a Cornwall Council planning committee.

The Trelion development would provide renewable energy equivalent to the average annual energy consumption of 9,500 homes and save approximately 21,000 tonnes of carbon dioxide emissions each year.

Planners note that the site is used for arable and grazing purposes, and state that the development would cause some landscape impact.

The application has received more than 135 comments on Cornwall Council's planning portal, with just two in support.

Cornwall councillor Elaine Kist (St Stephen-In-Brannel, Reform UK) shares the concerns of the parish council, which has opposed the plan.

She said: "These include conflict with the St Stephen-in-Brannel neighbourhood development plan, adverse landscape, visual and accumulative impacts, concerns about biodiversity net gain, flood risk, impacts on residential amenity and more."

Link to Image

The Stop Trelion Solar Farm group was formed to protest against the proposal. The members argue that their particular part of Cornwall is being "dumped on again". There are eight other solar farms near St Stephen due to the area's proximity to a National Grid connection.

The group carried out a mapping exercise which showed seven per cent of land in their area will be covered by solar panels; the average in Cornwall is 0.34 per cent.

Farmer Rose Barnecut said: "There's a solar park gold rush going on here and this Clay Country village community is just being asked to take too much.

"The really shocking thing is the proposed site will by EDF's own assessment be visible from a staggering 80 per cent of St Stephen parish. What people will see as they look across the landscape is behind them the clay waste and in front of them a sea of black glass."

The group supports the general development of renewable energy but members believe there are a large number of reasons why the Trelion solar farm should be refused.

value.Title: Indra Energy 's New, Renewable-Backed Plans Signal a Shift In How Homeowners Choose Clean Power

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-N3N3-RSVB-V2S9-00000-00Indra Energy's New, Renewable-Backed Plans Signal a Shift In How Homeowners Choose Clean Power2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSIndra Energy's New, Renewable-Backed Plans Signal a Shift In How Homeowners Choose Clean Power

Houston, TX, United States, May 11th, 2026, FinanceWire

Indra Energy, a provider of renewable energy plans, has announced an expanded focus on improving access to renewable energy for homeowners amid evolving market conditions. The development comes as deregulated energy markets and increasing demand for clean energy continue to influence how consumers source electricity.

The company stated that it is positioning its renewable-backed energy plans to address barriers to entry that have historically limited homeowner participation in renewable energy programs. By leveraging existing market structures, Indra Energy aims to offer alternatives that may broaden access without requiring significant infrastructure changes at the household level.

In a recent interview, Jeff Rodgers, Chief Marketing Officer at Indra Energy, discussed the company's approach to making renewable energy options more widely available. He outlined how the company's plan offerings are designed to align with changing consumer preferences and regulatory environments.

The Power of Choice

For many years, the majority of homeowners haven't really had a choice about where they get their power. In many cities and states across the country, customers are restricted to a single electricity supplier that has an essential monopoly over this service. For homeowners who want to switch to "clean" power sources, installing rooftop solar panels is the only option.

Entering energy deregulation - a system that has seen growing adoption across the country to give homeowners the ability to choose their energy supplier. Unlike areas that have a regulated monopoly, deregulated markets allow a variety of suppliers to compete in a particular market. According to Rodgers, market deregulation is an important first step in expanding clean energy access.

"With deregulated energy markets, homeowners are in control of choosing their power supplier - and that includes having the option to use clean energy providers," he says. "Deregulated markets tend to lead the way in clean energy adoption simply because there isn't a utility monopoly that controls everything. When people have the ability to choose, those who prioritize clean energy are going to make that a priority in their supplier search."

To that end, Indra Energy has begun introducing plans for electricity and natural gas in parts of the Northeast and Midwest U.S. that offer deregulated markets. Residents of Delaware, Indiana, Massachusetts, Michigan, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Virginia and the District of Columbia can receive electricity, natural gas or both utilities through Indra Energy.

No Solar Panels Required

Another long-standing belief regarding clean power is that it requires homeowners to install solar panels or other equipment to generate renewable energy on their own. While solar panels are growing in popularity, they are often inaccessible. A report by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory determined that 49% of households in the United States could not install a solar panel system because of insufficient roof space, insufficient roof access (such as in a multi-unit housing facility) or renting status.

Aside from these broad concerns, many homeowners simply can't afford to install solar panels because of financial constraints, while local building codes restricting panel placement can also limit energy generation capacity.

Rodgers and the Indra Energy team have opted for a simpler approach that streamlines clean energy access for homeowners. "Our electricity plans are backed by Renewable Energy Credits (RECs). These are issued to certify when one megawatt-hour of electricity has been generated by a renewable source like solar, wind or hydro," he explains."For our electric plans, Indra Energy purchases energy from RECs for the individual homeowners' energy usage. Similarly, our natural gas plans are paired with carbon offsets. We match natural gas use with verified offset projects designed to reduce or remove greenhouse gases so that you will have net-zero emissions from your energy use."Rodgers notes that this setup means homeowners aren't necessarily getting "green electrons" delivered directly to their home. "As an energy supplier, when we purchase RECs, it ensures that renewable energy is added to the grid in an amount that equals your usage. The electricity delivered to your home comes from the grid mix, which can include a mix of renewable and non-renewable sources. While you may not be directly getting renewables, you can have confidence that your usage has been matched with renewable energy, so you're still having a positive impact on the environment."

A Streamlined Way to Switch

With no additional equipment required, the process of switching to Indra Energy is surprisingly simple. When homeowners in deregulated markets select Indra Energy as their supplier, their electricity and natural gas continue to be delivered through existing utility lines.

While the supplier changes, the local utility still handles ongoing service, including system maintenance and repairs, meter reads and billing. Because energy is provided through existing lines, homeowners don't experience any interruption to their service.

"I hope this really changes the way people think about accessing renewable energy," Rodgers says. "It doesn't have to be this big expensive process that might not even work for your property. It can be as simple as signing up for a new energy provider and having access to clean energy, without any visible change to your home or daily routine. When accessibility becomes the standard, green energy adoption can increase dramatically and we can have a bigger collective impact on the environment."

As Rodgers and Indra Energy illustrate, how homeowners are accessing clean energy is rapidly changing. With more accessible solutions readily available, green energy is poised to expand beyond its relatively niche adoption rates into something that can be used by a broader range of homeowners than ever before.

About Indra Energy

Indra Energy is an independent, licensed supplier of renewable electricity and carbon-offset natural gas. Its residential energy plans help customers reduce their carbon footprint without installing solar panels by supplying electricity backed by Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) and offsetting all natural gas usage with carbon credits. Indra Energy currently offers plans across seven markets and 39 utilities in Delaware, Massachusetts, Michigan, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Virginia and the District of Columbia.

Contact

Indra Energy

hello@indraenergy.com

Disclaimer. This is a paid press release.

value.Title: Microsoft Considers Adjusting 2030 Renewable Energy Goal Amid AI Demand

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHV-CXJ3-RTBH-G008-00000-00Microsoft Considers Adjusting 2030 Renewable Energy Goal Amid AI Demand2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisMicrosoft Considers Adjusting 2030 Renewable Energy Goal Amid AI Demand

AMERICAN DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

07 May 2026 12:15 EDT

Microsoft Corporation is contemplating the possibility of delaying or potentially abandoning its ambitious goal to match its entire hourly electricity use with renewable energy purchases by 2030. The discussions, which are ongoing, have emerged on 7th May 2026, as the company's focus on expanding its data center capabilities in response to surging demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure reshapes the feasibility of its climate commitments.

The shift towards energy-intensive data centers has raised questions about Microsoft’s previous climate pledges, which were considered among the most ambitious in the tech industry. A spokesperson for Microsoft stated that the company is still exploring avenues to uphold its matching goal, highlighting recent agreements with We Energies aimed at integrating 1.2 gigawatts of carbon-free energy projects into the grid by December 2028.

Like its competitors Amazon and Alphabet, Microsoft is investing heavily in artificial intelligence infrastructure to support services such as its Copilot assistant and Azure cloud offerings. The new data centers being developed are projected to require multiple gigawatts of energy, with a single gigawatt capable of powering approximately 750,000 U.S. homes. This surge in energy demand has led to a spate of deals, including collaborations focused on nuclear energy, as well as increased reliance on natural gas, which some executives argue can be deployed more quickly than renewable sources.

In 2024, Microsoft entered into a power agreement with Constellation Energy aimed at reviving a unit of the Three Mile Island nuclear plant in Pennsylvania. This move underscores the company's shift in strategy as it navigates the complexities of balancing its energy needs with its sustainability commitments amid the rapid growth of AI technology.

As Microsoft evaluates its options, the question remains whether it can maintain its ambitious renewable energy targets while accommodating the escalating energy demands associated with its AI ventures. Investors will be closely monitoring the company's decisions as they could have significant implications for its long-term sustainability goals.

SECTION 2 MICROSOFT ACTIVITIES

Activities

Microsoft Corporation, founded in 1975 and headquartered in Redmond, Washington, is a global leader in technology, developing and licensing software, hardware, and cloud-based services. Its core business activities encompass operating systems like Windows, productivity tools such as Microsoft Office and Teams, enterprise solutions including Azure cloud computing, and hardware devices like Surface. The company also operates in gaming through Xbox and professional networking via LinkedIn. Among its fastest-growing segments are cloud services, particularly Azure, which has expanded rapidly due to rising demand for scalable computing, AI-driven solutions, and hybrid cloud offerings, contributing significantly to revenue growth. Additionally, subscriptions for productivity software and emerging AI integrations, such as in Copilot, are fueling innovation and market expansion, solidifying Microsoft's position in the digital transformation landscape. With a strong focus on security and sustainability, Microsoft continues to drive technological advancements for businesses and consumers worldwide. It is the NASDAQ's largest Computer software company by market capitalisation.
